---
title: "Lab 1: Development Environment Setup"
date: "2026-01-20"
due: "2026-01-28"
---

# Lab 1: Development Environment Setup {.page-break-before}

Welcome to your first web development lab! This lab will guide you through setting up a professional development environment for web development.

## Lab Objectives {.objectives}

By the end of this lab, you will be able to:

::: {.objectives-list}
- вң“ Install and configure Visual Studio Code (VS Code)
- вң“ Set up essential VS Code extensions for web development
- вң“ Create a proper project folder structure
- вң“ Write and view your first HTML file
- вң“ Use browser developer tools effectively
:::

## Pre-Lab Preparation {.preparation}

### What You Need

::: {.callout-note}
Before you start, make sure you have the following:

- A computer with **Windows**, **macOS**, or **Linux**
- **Administrator access** to install software
- **Internet connection** for downloading software
- At least **2GB of free disk space**
:::

### Estimated Time

**60-90 minutes** (depending on download speeds and your familiarity with software installation)

---

## Part 1: Install Visual Studio Code {.part-header}

Visual Studio Code is a lightweight but powerful code editor perfect for web development.

### Step 1: Download VS Code

1. Open your web browser
2. Visit: [https://code.visualstudio.com/](https://code.visualstudio.com/)
3. Click the download button for your operating system
4. Wait for the installer to download (usually 50-100 MB)

### Step 2: Install VS Code

#### **Windows:**

1. Run the downloaded `.exe` file
2. Accept the User Account Control prompt
3. Accept the license agreement
4. Choose installation location (default is fine)
5. **Important**: Check these options:
   - вҳ‘ "Add to PATH"
   - вҳ‘ "Create a desktop icon"
   - вҳ‘ "Add 'Open with Code' action to context menu"
6. Click **Install**
7. Click **Finish**

::: {.callout-tip}
Adding VS Code to PATH allows you to open directories in VS Code from the command line using `code .`
:::

#### **macOS:**

1. Open the downloaded `.dmg` file
2. Drag the **VS Code icon** to the **Applications folder**
3. Wait for the copy to complete
4. Open **Applications** folder
5. Double-click **Visual Studio Code**

#### **Linux:**

Visit the [VS Code website](https://code.visualstudio.com/#alt-downloads) and follow the instructions for your specific distribution.

### Step 3: First Launch

1. Open **Visual Studio Code**
2. You should see the Welcome tab
3. Familiarize yourself with the interface:

::: {.layout-columns}

**Activity Bar (Left side):**
- File Explorer
- Search
- Source Control
- Run and Debug
- Extensions

**Main Editor Area (Center):**
- Where your code appears
- Tabs for multiple files
- Line numbers on the left

**Side Bar:**
- File browser
- Search results
- Git information
- Installed extensions

**Status Bar (Bottom):**
- File info (encoding, language, line count)
- Current branch
- Errors and warnings count

:::

---

## Part 2: Install Essential Extensions {.part-header}

Extensions supercharge VS Code by adding features specific to web development. Think of them as plugins!

### Step 1: Open Extensions View

1. Click the **Extensions icon** in the Activity Bar (looks like 4 small squares)
2. Or press: `Ctrl+Shift+X` (Windows/Linux) or `Cmd+Shift+X` (Mac)
3. You'll see a search bar at the top

### Step 2: Install Required Extensions

Search for each extension and click **Install**:

:::: {.extension-list}

::: {.extension-card}
**рҹҡҖ Live Server** *(by Ritwick Dey)*

Creates a local development server with live reload functionality.

**Why you need it:** Instantly see changes in your browser without manual refresh.
:::

::: {.extension-card}
**рҹҺЁ HTML CSS Support** *(by ecmel)*

Provides HTML and CSS IntelliSense and code completion.

**Why you need it:** Auto-suggests HTML tags and CSS properties as you type.
:::

::: {.extension-card}
**рҹ”„ Auto Rename Tag** *(by Jun Han)*

Automatically renames paired HTML tags when you edit one.

**Why you need it:** Prevents mismatched closing tags.
:::

::: {.extension-card}
**вңЁ Prettier - Code formatter** *(by Prettier)*

Automatically formats your code consistently.

**Why you need it:** Makes code readable and follows best practices.
:::

::: {.extension-card}
**рҹ“Ӯ Path Intellisense** *(by Christian Kohler)*

Auto-completes file paths as you type.

**Why you need it:** Helps link images, stylesheets, and other files correctly.
:::

::: {.extension-card .optional}
**рҹҺҜ Bracket Pair Colorizer 2** *(by CoenraadS)* - **OPTIONAL**

Colors matching brackets in different colors.

**Why you might want it:** Makes nested code easier to read.
:::

::::

### Step 3: Configure Prettier (Code Formatter)

After installing Prettier, configure it for automatic formatting:

1. Press `Ctrl+,` (Windows/Linux) or `Cmd+,` (Mac) to open Settings
2. Search for: **"format on save"**
3. Check the checkbox for **"Editor: Format On Save"**
4. Now your code automatically formats when you save!

::: {.callout-success}
**Pro Tip:** Your code will be automatically formatted to follow best practices every time you save. This is incredibly helpful!
:::

---

## Part 3: Create Project Structure {.part-header}

Proper file organization is crucial for successful web development. Let's create a well-structured project folder.

### Step 1: Create Main Folder

Create a folder on your computer for this course. Choose an appropriate location like your Documents folder:

**Windows:**
```
C:\Users\[YourUsername]\Documents\BMC201-WebTech\
```

**macOS:**
```
/Users/[YourUsername]/Documents/BMC201-WebTech/
```

**Linux:**
```
/home/[username]/Documents/BMC201-WebTech/
```

### Step 2: Create Subfolders

Inside `BMC201-WebTech`, create these subfolders:

```
BMC201-WebTech/
в”Ӯ
в”ңв”Җв”Җ Labs/
в”Ӯ   в”ңв”Җв”Җ Lab01/
в”Ӯ   в”ңв”Җв”Җ Lab02/
в”Ӯ   в”ңв”Җв”Җ Lab03/
в”Ӯ   в””в”Җв”Җ ...
в”Ӯ
в”ңв”Җв”Җ Assignments/
в”Ӯ   в”ңв”Җв”Җ Assignment01/
в”Ӯ   в”ңв”Җв”Җ Assignment02/
в”Ӯ   в””в”Җв”Җ ...
в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җ Practice/
    в””в”Җв”Җ (for your own practice exercises)
```

::: {.callout-note}
**Why this structure?**
- Keeps course materials organized
- Prevents files from getting mixed up
- Makes it easy to find assignments
- Professional practice for real projects
:::

### Step 3: Open Folder in VS Code

1. Open **Visual Studio Code**
2. Go to **File вҶ’ Open Folder**
3. Navigate to your `BMC201-WebTech` folder
4. Click **Select Folder**
5. The entire folder now appears in your Explorer panel
6. You'll see all your subfolders on the left

::: {.callout-success}
Congratulations! You now have VS Code working as your project workspace. All files created here will be accessible from the Explorer.
:::

---

## Part 4: Create Your First HTML File {.part-header}

Now let's create your first HTML file using VS Code's built-in Emmet tool for rapid HTML generation.

### Step 1: Create the HTML File

1. In VS Code's Explorer panel, right-click on the **`Labs/Lab01/`** folder
2. Select **"New File"**
3. Name the file: **`index.html`**
4. Press Enter

::: {.callout-note}
**Why `index.html`?**
This is the default filename web servers look for. Every website folder should have an `index.html` file.
:::

### Step 2: Generate HTML Structure with Emmet

VS Code includes Emmet, which creates HTML boilerplate instantly!

1. Click inside the empty `index.html` file
2. Type: `!` (just the exclamation mark)
3. Press `Tab` or `Enter`
4. VS Code generates a complete HTML5 template!

You should see:

```html
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
    <meta charset="UTF-8">
    <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">
    <title>Document</title>
</head>
<body>
    
</body>
</html>
```

::: {.callout-success}
**This is Emmet magic!** Instead of typing all that, one character did it. Emmet has many more shortcuts like this.
:::

### Step 3: Customize Your HTML Page

Replace "Document" with a descriptive title and add your content:

```html
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
    <meta charset="UTF-8">
    <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">
    <title>My First Web Page - Lab 1</title>
</head>
<body>
    <h1>Welcome to Web Technology!</h1>
    
    <h2>About Me</h2>
    <p>
        My name is [Your Name] and I am a student in the BMC201 Web Technology 
        course at Galgotias College of Engineering & Technology.
    </p>
    
    <h2>Course Information</h2>
    <ul>
        <li>Course Code: BMC201</li>
        <li>Course Title: Web Technology</li>
        <li>Semester: MCA Semester 2</li>
        <li>Academic Year: 2025-26</li>
    </ul>
    
    <h2>What I Hope to Learn</h2>
    <ol>
        <li>HTML5 and semantic markup</li>
        <li>CSS3 for styling and responsive layouts</li>
        <li>JavaScript for interactivity and DOM manipulation</li>
        <li>Web server configuration and deployment</li>
        <li>Modern web development practices</li>
    </ol>
    
    <h2>Contact Information</h2>
    <p>
        Email: <a href="mailto:your.email@galgotiacollege.edu">your.email@galgotiacollege.edu</a><br>
        Roll Number: [Your Roll Number]<br>
        College: Galgotias College of Engineering & Technology
    </p>
    
    <footer>
        <hr>
        <p>Created on: [Today's Date]</p>
        <p>&copy; 2026 [Your Name]. All rights reserved.</p>
    </footer>
</body>
</html>
```

::: {.callout-warning}
**Before saving:** Replace all items in [square brackets] with your actual information!
:::

### Step 4: Save Your File

1. Press `Ctrl+S` (Windows/Linux) or `Cmd+S` (Mac)
2. The white dot next to the filename disappears (indicating it's saved)

---

## Part 5: View Your Web Page {.part-header}

Time to see your HTML rendered in a browser! There are multiple ways to do this.

### Method 1: Using Live Server (Recommended!)

Live Server is the extension you installed. It creates a local web server with live reload.

1. Open your `index.html` file in VS Code
2. Right-click anywhere in the file
3. Select **"Open with Live Server"**
4. Your default browser opens automatically to `http://localhost:5500`
5. You should see your formatted web page!

::: {.callout-success}
**Live Reload Magic:** Now try editing your HTML (change some text, add a new paragraph). Your browser automatically reloads as soon as you save! No manual refresh needed.
:::

**To stop Live Server:**
- Close the browser tab, or
- In VS Code, click "Go Live" button in the bottom status bar

### Method 2: Direct File Opening

If Live Server isn't working for some reason:

1. In VS Code, right-click `index.html` in Explorer
2. Select **"Reveal in File Explorer"** (Windows) or **"Reveal in Finder"** (macOS)
3. Double-click the `index.html` file
4. It opens in your default browser

::: {.callout-note}
**Difference:** Manual file opening doesn't have live reload. You must refresh manually with **F5**.
:::

### Method 3: Browser Address Bar

You can also manually navigate:

1. Open your browser
2. Press `Ctrl+L` (Windows) or `Cmd+L` (Mac) to focus the address bar
3. Type the full path to your file:
   ```
   file:///C:/Users/YourName/Documents/BMC201-WebTech/Labs/Lab01/index.html
   ```
4. Press Enter

---

## Part 6: Explore Browser Developer Tools {.part-header}

Browser Developer Tools are essential for web development. They let you inspect, debug, and test your code directly in the browser.

### Step 1: Open Developer Tools

Open Developer Tools using one of these methods:

**Google Chrome / Microsoft Edge / Brave:**
- Press: `F12`, or
- Press: `Ctrl+Shift+I` (Windows) or `Cmd+Option+I` (Mac), or
- Right-click anywhere on the page вҶ’ **"Inspect"**

**Firefox:**
- Press: `F12`, or
- Right-click вҶ’ **"Inspect Element"**

**Safari:**
- First enable Developer Menu: Preferences вҶ’ Advanced вң“ "Show Develop menu"
- Press: `Cmd+Option+I`

### Step 2: Explore the Key Tabs

Developer Tools have several important tabs. Let's explore the main ones:

#### **1. Elements/Inspector Tab** рҹ”Қ

This shows the HTML structure of your page.

```html
<h1>Welcome to Web Technology!</h1>
```

::: {.lab-action}
**Try this:**
1. Click the **Inspector/Elements** tab (should be default)
2. Find the `<h1>` element in the code
3. Right-click it and select **"Edit as HTML"**
4. Change the text to something else
5. Press Enter
6. Your page updates! (but not the actual file)
:::

::: {.callout-warning}
**Important:** These changes are temporary! They only affect what you see in the browser. Your actual HTML file is unchanged. Refresh the page to see the original again.
:::

#### **2. Console Tab** рҹ’»

The Console lets you interact with JavaScript (we'll use this more later).

::: {.lab-action}
**Try this:**
1. Click the **Console** tab
2. You should see a `>` prompt
3. Type: `console.log("Hello from Lab 1!")`
4. Press Enter
5. You'll see your message printed!

This is useful for testing JavaScript code.
:::

#### **3. Network Tab** рҹҢҗ

Shows all files downloaded when loading the page (HTML, CSS, images, etc.).

::: {.lab-action}
**Try this:**
1. Click the **Network** tab
2. Refresh the page (`F5` or `Ctrl+R`)
3. You'll see all files loaded:
   - `index.html` (main document)
   - CSS files (styling)
   - JavaScript files
   - Images
4. Click `index.html` to see request/response details
:::

#### **4. Application / Storage Tab** рҹ’ҫ

Shows local storage, cookies, session data (we'll use this later in the course).

### Step 3: Practice with the Inspector

Let's do some hands-on experiments:

::: {.lab-action}
**Experiment 1: Edit HTML**
1. Open Inspector tab
2. Find your `<h1>` element
3. Double-click to edit
4. Change the text and press Enter
5. Notice on the page: Text changed!
6. Refresh page: Changes gone (file was never modified)
:::

::: {.lab-action}
**Experiment 2: Hide Elements**
1. Find any element in the Inspector
2. Right-click вҶ’ "Hide element"
3. It disappears from page view
4. Refresh: It's back!
:::

::: {.lab-action}
**Experiment 3: Change Styles (Preview)**
1. Find any element in Inspector
2. Look at the "Styles" panel on the right
3. Try toggling styles on/off
4. Hover over rules to see effects
:::

::: {.callout-success}
**Why this matters:** Developer Tools let you instantly test changes without editing files. Perfect for experimenting and debugging!
:::

---

## Part 7: Lab Questions {.part-header}

Answer these questions in a document (Word `.docx` or text `.txt`). These answers test your understanding of the concepts covered.

### Questions

::: {.qa-section}

**Q1: HTML Document Declaration**

What is the purpose of the `<!DOCTYPE html>` declaration? Why is it important to include it at the beginning of every HTML5 file?

*Hint: What does the browser do with this information?*

---

**Q2: Language Attribute**

Why is the `lang="en"` attribute important in the `<html>` tag? What are the consequences of omitting it?

*Hint: Think about accessibility and search engines.*

---

**Q3: Character Encoding**

What does the `<meta charset="UTF-8">` tag do? Why should you include UTF-8 encoding in your HTML?

*Hint: What characters does UTF-8 support?*

---

**Q4: HTML Lists**

Explain the difference between `<ul>` (unordered list) and `<ol>` (ordered list). Provide an example of when you would use each one. What HTML element do you use for each list item?

*Hint: ul = bullet points, ol = numbered items*

---

**Q5: Browser Developer Tools Behavior**

When you edited your HTML in the browser's Developer Tools (Inspector), what happened? Did it change your actual HTML file? Why or why not? What happens when you refresh?

*Hint: Developer Tools work on the live page, not the actual file.*

---

**Q6: VS Code Extensions**

Name at least three VS Code extensions you installed and briefly explain what each one does and why it's useful for web development.

*Hint: Think about Live Server, Prettier, HTML CSS Support, etc.*

---

**Q7: Keyboard Shortcuts**

List at least five keyboard shortcuts you learned and used during this lab. Include both VS Code and browser shortcuts.

**Examples:**
- VS Code shortcuts: File operations, formatting, etc.
- Browser shortcuts: Opening DevTools, refresh, etc.

---

:::

## Part 8: Bonus Challenges {.part-header}

These optional challenges will deepen your understanding and help you practice HTML:

::: {.challenge-section}

**рҹҢҹ Challenge 1: Add a Weekly Schedule Table**

Create an HTML table showing your weekly schedule. Include:
- Days of the week as headers (Monday, Tuesday, etc.)
- Time slots
- Your classes/activities
- Use proper `<table>`, `<tr>`, `<td>`, `<th>` tags

**Example:**
```html
<table border="1">
    <tr>
        <th>Time</th>
        <th>Monday</th>
        <th>Tuesday</th>
    </tr>
    <tr>
        <td>9:00 AM</td>
        <td>BMC201</td>
        <td>Lab</td>
    </tr>
</table>
```

---

**рҹ–јпёҸ Challenge 2: Add Images to Your Page**

1. Create an `images/` folder in `Lab01/`
2. Download 3-5 images from [Unsplash](https://unsplash.com/) or [Pixabay](https://pixabay.com/)
3. Save them in your `images/` folder
4. Add them to your HTML using `<img>` tags
5. Include `alt` text for accessibility

**Tips:**
- Use relative paths: `<img src="images/photo.jpg">`
- Add `width="300"` to control size
- Every image needs `alt="description"`

---

**рҹ”— Challenge 3: Create a Multi-Page Website**

1. Create a second page: `about.html`
2. Add content about web development
3. Create a `portfolio.html` page showing your projects
4. Add navigation links between pages

**Example:**
```html
<nav>
    <a href="index.html">Home</a>
    <a href="about.html">About</a>
    <a href="portfolio.html">Portfolio</a>
</nav>
```

---

**рҹ’¬ Challenge 4: Add Comments to Your HTML**

HTML comments help document your code. Add comments explaining each section:

```html
<!-- Header with title -->
<h1>Welcome to Web Technology!</h1>

<!-- Information about the course -->
<h2>Course Information</h2>
<ul>
    <li>Course Code: BMC201</li>
    <!-- ... more content ... -->
</ul>
```

Comments don't display on the page but help you and other developers understand the code.

---

**вң… Challenge 5: Validate Your HTML**

Use the W3C HTML Validator to check for errors:

1. Visit [https://validator.w3.org/](https://validator.w3.org/)
2. Click "Validate by File Upload"
3. Select your `index.html` file
4. Fix any errors or warnings
5. Repeat until you have valid HTML!

**Why validate?**
- Ensures your HTML follows standards
- Catches typos and syntax errors
- Improves compatibility across browsers
- Better accessibility

---

:::

## Submission Requirements {.submission-header}

### What to Submit

Create a folder structure and include all the following:

```
[YourRollNumber]_Lab01_[YourName]/
в”Ӯ
в”ңв”Җв”Җ index.html                    # Your HTML file
в”ңв”Җв”Җ images/                       # Folder with images (if you used them)
в”ңв”Җв”Җ Screenshots/                  # Folder with required screenshots
в”Ӯ   в”ңв”Җв”Җ 01-VSCode-setup.png
в”Ӯ   в”ңв”Җв”Җ 02-webpage-browser.png
в”Ӯ   в”ңв”Җв”Җ 03-devtools-open.png
в”Ӯ   в””в”Җв”Җ 04-validated.png
в”Ӯ
в”ңв”Җв”Җ Answers.txt                   # Answers to lab questions
в”ңв”Җв”Җ BONUS_Challenges.txt          # Bonus challenges (if attempted)
в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җ README.txt                    # Project information
```

### Screenshots Required

Take screenshots showing:

1. **VS Code Setup:** Explorer with folder structure visible
2. **Webpage in Browser:** Your HTML rendered in browser
3. **Developer Tools:** DevTools open showing Inspector
4. **W3C Validation:** Your HTML validation report (if completing Challenge 5)

::: {.callout-note}
Use the `Print Screen` key or `Shift+Print Screen` to capture screenshots, then paste into Paint or Word.
:::

### README.txt Template

```
Lab 1: Development Environment Setup
=====================================

Student Name: [Your Full Name]
Roll Number: [Your Roll Number]
Email: [Your Email]

Date Completed: [MM/DD/2026]
Time Spent: [X hours Y minutes]

Environment Used:
- Operating System: [Windows/macOS/Linux]
- VS Code Version: [Check Help вҶ’ About]
- Browser: [Chrome/Firefox/Edge/Safari]

Issues Encountered:
[Describe any problems and how you solved them]

Additional Notes:
[Any other relevant information]
```

### Create ZIP File

1. Compress your entire folder into a ZIP file
2. Name it: **`[RollNumber]_Lab01_[YourName].zip`**
   - Example: `MCA12345_Lab01_RahulKumar.zip`

### Submit

::: {.callout-warning}
**Due Date: January 28, 2026, 11:59 PM**

Submit via: [Classroom Portal/Email] to [instructor-email@galgotiacollege.edu](mailto:prashantkumar.nag@galgotiacollege.edu)
:::

---

## Grading Rubric {.grading-header}

Your submission will be evaluated on the following criteria:

| # | Criteria | Points | Notes |
|---|----------|--------|-------|
| 1 | **Environment Setup** | 15 | VS Code installed and configured properly with correct extensions |
| 2 | **Extensions Installation** | 10 | All 5+ required extensions installed and working |
| 3 | **Project Structure** | 10 | Proper folder organization (Labs/, Assignments/, etc.) |
| 4 | **HTML File Creation** | 20 | Valid HTML5 with all required content sections |
| 5 | **Screenshots** | 15 | All 4 screenshots clear and showing required elements |
| 6 | **Lab Questions** | 15 | All 7 questions answered completely and correctly |
| 7 | **Bonus Challenges** | 10 | Optional: Extra credit for attempting challenges |
| 8 | **Submission Format** | 5 | Proper ZIP naming and file organization |
| **TOTAL** | | **100** | |

### Grading Scale

- **90-100:** Excellent - Complete, well-organized, shows deep understanding
- **80-89:** Good - Complete with minor issues, solid understanding
- **70-79:** Satisfactory - Mostly complete, some gaps in understanding
- **60-69:** Needs Improvement - Significant issues or missing content
- **Below 60:** Incomplete - Missing major components

---

## Troubleshooting Guide {.troubleshooting-header}

Encountering issues? Try these solutions:

### Live Server Issues

::: {.troubleshooting-item}

**Problem:** "Live Server" option doesn't appear in right-click menu

**Solutions:**
1. Make sure Live Server extension is installed (check Extensions)
2. Reload VS Code: `Ctrl+Shift+P` вҶ’ type "reload window" вҶ’ press Enter
3. Restart VS Code completely
4. Make sure you're right-clicking inside an HTML file, not the folder

:::

::: {.troubleshooting-item}

**Problem:** Page doesn't update automatically when I save

**Solutions:**
1. Verify Live Server shows "Go Live" in status bar (means it's running)
2. Check that "Format on Save" isn't causing issues (temporarily disable)
3. Save with `Ctrl+S` explicitly
4. Hard refresh browser: `Ctrl+F5` (Windows) or `Cmd+Shift+R` (Mac)

:::

::: {.troubleshooting-item}

**Problem:** Can't see changes in the browser even after refresh

**Solutions:**
1. Make sure you saved the file (check for white dot next to filename)
2. Hard refresh: `Ctrl+F5` or `Ctrl+Shift+R`
3. Clear browser cache: Settings вҶ’ Clear browsing data
4. Close and reopen Live Server

:::

### Extension Issues

::: {.troubleshooting-item}

**Problem:** Extensions not working after installation

**Solutions:**
1. Reload VS Code window (`Ctrl+Shift+P` вҶ’ "reload window")
2. Check that extension is **Enabled** in Extensions panel
3. Restart your computer
4. Uninstall and reinstall the extension

:::

::: {.troubleshooting-item}

**Problem:** Prettier formatting makes code look weird

**Solutions:**
1. Press `Ctrl+,` to open Settings
2. Search "editor word wrap"
3. Set to "on"
4. Check Prettier settings: Search "prettier"

:::

### Developer Tools Issues

::: {.troubleshooting-item}

**Problem:** Developer Tools won't open

**Solutions:**
1. Try: `F12` key
2. Try: `Ctrl+Shift+I` (Windows) or `Cmd+Option+I` (Mac)
3. Try: Right-click вҶ’ "Inspect Element"
4. Restart browser completely

:::

### File Path Issues

::: {.troubleshooting-item}

**Problem:** "File not found" or browser shows blank page

**Solutions:**
1. Check file path in `<img>` tags uses forward slashes `/`
2. Use relative paths: `images/photo.jpg` (not `C:/path/...`)
3. Verify file exists in that location
4. Check spelling (case-sensitive on Linux/Mac)

:::

### Installation Issues

::: {.troubleshooting-item}

**Problem:** Can't install VS Code (permission denied)

**Solutions:**
1. Run installer as Administrator (Windows)
2. On Mac: Drag to Applications folder (not a click and open)
3. On Linux: Use `sudo apt install code` after adding repository

:::

### Still Having Problems?

::: {.callout-warning}

If none of these solutions work:

1. **Check the Logs:**
   - VS Code: View вҶ’ Output вҶ’ Choose "Log" from dropdown
   - Look for red error messages

2. **Search Online:**
   - Copy error message into Google
   - Try Stack Overflow: https://stackoverflow.com/

3. **Ask for Help:**
   - Post in course discussion forum
   - Email: [prashantkumar.nag@galgotiacollege.edu](mailto:prashantkumar.nag@galgotiacollege.edu)
   - Include: Your OS, error message, what you tried

:::

---

## Additional Resources {.resources-header}

Here are excellent resources for learning more:

### Official Documentation

- **[Visual Studio Code Docs](https://code.visualstudio.com/docs)** - Official VS Code documentation and guides
- **[VS Code Keyboard Shortcuts](https://code.visualstudio.com/shortcuts/keyboard-shortcuts-windows.pdf)** - Downloadable PDF of all shortcuts (also for Mac and Linux)
- **[MDN Learning Resources](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Learn/)** - Comprehensive web development tutorials

### HTML Learning

- **[MDN: HTML Introduction](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Learn/HTML/Introduction_to_HTML/Getting_started)** - Excellent HTML primer
- **[W3Schools HTML Tutorial](https://www.w3schools.com/html/)** - Interactive HTML learning
- **[HTML Living Standard](https://html.spec.whatwg.org/)** - Official HTML specification

### Browser Developer Tools

- **[Chrome DevTools Docs](https://developer.chrome.com/docs/devtools/)** - Complete DevTools guide
- **[Firefox Developer Tools](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Tools)** - Firefox DevTools documentation

### Validation & Best Practices

- **[W3C HTML Validator](https://validator.w3.org/)** - Check your HTML for errors
- **[Can I Use](https://caniuse.com/)** - Browser support for HTML features
- **[Web Accessibility Guidelines](https://www.w3.org/WAI/)** - Learn about accessible web design

### Course Resources

- **[BMC201 Schedule](/schedule.qmd)** - Course schedule and all materials
- **[BMC201 Content](/content/index.qmd)** - Lecture notes and materials
- **[Course Resources](/resource/index.qmd)** - Additional learning materials

---

## Questions & Support {.support-header}

### Getting Help

::: {.callout-note}

**During Lab Sessions:**
- Ask your lab instructor directly
- Clarify any part of these instructions
- Work through issues together

**Outside Lab:**
- Email your instructor: [prashantkumar.nag@galgotiacollege.edu](mailto:prashantkumar.nag@galgotiacollege.edu)
- Subject line: `[BMC201] Lab 1: [Your Question]`
- Include: What you're trying to do, error message, what you've tried

**Course Forum:**
- Check the course discussion forum
- Others may have had the same issue
- Instructors monitor and respond

**Office Hours:**
- Schedule a meeting with your instructor
- Get personalized help
- Discuss challenges or advanced topics

:::

### Providing Good Bug Reports

When reporting an issue, include:

1. **Your Environment:** OS, VS Code version, extensions
2. **What You're Trying to Do:** Clear description
3. **What Happened:** The error or unexpected behavior
4. **What You Expected:** What should have happened
5. **What You've Tried:** Solutions you've already attempted
6. **Screenshots/Logs:** Error messages or screenshots

**Example:** "On Windows 10, Live Server doesn't launch a browser. I've restarted VS Code and tried re-installing the extension. Error log shows: ..."

---

## Lab Summary {.summary-header}

### What You've Accomplished

Congratulations! In this lab, you've:

вң… Set up a professional development environment
вң… Installed and configured VS Code
вң… Added essential web development extensions
вң… Created proper project structure
вң… Written your first HTML5 page
вң… Learned to use browser Developer Tools
вң… Practiced debugging web pages

### Next Steps

*Lab 2* will build on this foundation:
- Create more complex HTML pages
- Use semantic HTML5 elements
- Add multimedia (images, audio, video)
- Create multi-page websites
- Practice form creation

Keep your project folder organized and your code clean. Good habits now make advanced topics easier!

---

## Lab Completion Checklist {.checklist-header}

Before submitting, verify you have:

- [ ] VS Code installed and working
- [ ] All 5+ extensions installed
- [ ] Project folder structure created
- [ ] `index.html` file created with content
- [ ] Your HTML file displays in browser
- [ ] Developer Tools working
- [ ] All lab questions answered
- [ ] Screenshots taken
- [ ] README.txt file created
- [ ] ZIP file created with correct name
- [ ] Everything ready for submission

---

**Congratulations on completing Lab 1! рҹҺү**

You're now ready for Lab 2. Keep practicing and experimenting!
